prior.title: EIA Open Data Portal - U.S. Energy Information Administration
prior.summary: The EIA Open Data platform provides access to official U.S. government energy statistics, data, analysis and forecasting resources. This is a public data repository maintained by the Energy Information Administration offering comprehensive energy-related datasets.
prior.full_brief:
# EIA Open Data Portal — U.S. Energy Information Administration

## Technology Profile

---

# 1. What's Being Tested or Deployed and By Whom

MONITORING

**What the platform is and what it provides.** The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) operates a free, publicly accessible Open Data Portal — the primary U.S. government source for energy statistics — delivering time-series data across all major energy categories via a multi-modal access architecture: a RESTful Application Programming Interface (APIv2), an Excel add-in, bulk file downloads, and embeddable widgets.

*Source: EIA Open Data Portal, U.S. Energy Information Administration, 2026. https://eia.gov/opendata

**API versioning status.** The API is actively maintained. The current version is APIv2.1.12, patched March 2026 with performance improvements and bug fixes. The immediately prior version, v2.1.11, was released January 2026, introducing improved date-range and frequency logic. The API has been in continuous public release since February 2022 (v2.0.1 Community Release) and public release since May 2022 (v2.0.2). APIv1, originally launched in 2012, is retained for backward compatibility via automatic series-ID translation.

*Source: EIA API Technical Documentation, U.S. Energy Information Administration, March 2026. https://www.eia.gov/opendata/documentation.php

**Data categories available.** The portal organises time-series data into the following categories relevant to freight sustainability workflows:

- **Petroleum & Other Liquids** — daily, weekly, monthly, and annual prices for retail gasoline and on-highway diesel fuel, spot and futures prices, distillate fuel oil consumption and sales, crude oil production and reserves, refining and processing, imports/exports, and stocks. *Source: EIA Open Data Portal, U.S. Energy Information Administration, 2026. https://eia.gov/opendata
- **CO₂ Emissions** — CO₂ emissions aggregates, carbon coefficients by fuel, state, and sector. *Source: PUDL/Catalyst Cooperative — EIA Bulk API Data Documentation, Catalyst Cooperative, 2026. https://catalystcoop-pudl.readthedocs.io/en/latest/data_sources/eiaapi.html
- **State Energy Data System (SEDS)** — annual state and U.S.-level data by energy source and sector in Btu units, covering production, consumption, prices, and expenditures from 1960 forward. *Source: EIA Open Data Portal, U.S. Energy Information Administration, 2026. https://eia.gov/opendata
- **Transportation energy consumption** — sector-specific energy use and fuel-mix data disaggregated by mode. *Source: EIA — Use of Energy for Transportation, U.S. Energy Information Administration, 2026. https://www.eia.gov/energyexplained/use-of-energy/transportation.php
- **Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO)** — monthly 18-month forward projections using the STEO model; Annual Energy Outlook — annual U.S. projections; International Energy Outlook — annual international projections. *Source: EIA Open Data Portal, U.S. Energy Information Administration, 2026. https://eia.gov/opendata

**Bulk data scale.** The EIA bulk electricity API data alone contains more than 750,000 JSON objects, most of which are time series. The total bulk download size is 1,526 MB.

*Source: PUDL/Catalyst Cooperative — EIA Bulk API Data Documentation, Catalyst Cooperative, 2026. https://catalystcoop-pudl.readthedocs.io/en/latest/data_sources/eiaapi.html

**Third-party integration evidence.** The Catalyst Cooperative PUDL project (an independent academic/NGO analytical pipeline) archives the full EIA bulk API and integrates specific fuel receipt and cost series, confirming that the API is operationally integrated into external sustainability analytics workflows beyond direct EIA access.

*Source: PUDL/Catalyst Cooperative — EIA Bulk API Data Documentation, Catalyst Cooperative, 2026. https://catalystcoop-pudl.readthedocs.io/en/latest/data_sources/eiaapi.html

**Key transportation energy baseline (2025 data).** Petroleum products accounted for approximately 89% of U.S. transportation energy in 2025. Distillate fuels — primarily diesel — accounted for 22% of transportation energy, and biofuels accounted for 6%.

*Source: EIA — Use of Energy for Transportation, U.S. Energy Information Administration, 2026. https://www.eia.gov/energyexplained/use-of-energy/transportation.php

*Specific [current on-highway diesel price figure] not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-05.* Weekly on-highway diesel prices are published via the Gasoline and Diesel Fuel Update (most recent release dated June 2, 2026), but the specific price value was not reproduced in the source blocks provided.

*Source: EIA Petroleum & Other Liquids Data, U.S. Energy Information Administration, June 2026. https://www.eia.gov/petroleum/data.php

---

# 2. What This Tells Us About Industry Trajectory

**Pattern: open government data becoming foundational infrastructure for freight emissions compliance.**

Analytical inference: The EIA Open Data Portal is not itself a compliance tool, but its data outputs — weekly diesel prices, state-level CO₂ emission factors, transportation sector fuel-mix shares, and forward price projections — are the primary U.S. government inputs into freight emissions accounting, cost modelling, and sustainability reporting workflows. As sustainability reporting frameworks (including CSRD, ISO 14083, and U.S. SEC climate disclosure rules) require granular, verifiable emissions intensity data at route and fuel-type level, the EIA portal's status as a free, API-accessible, government-authoritative source positions it as a backbone data layer for compliance, rather than a supplementary reference.

Analytical inference: The active maintenance cadence — four API version patches between September 2025 and March 2026 — signals that EIA is actively investing in the portal's technical reliability, not treating it as a static archive. This is relevant because data pipeline stability is a prerequisite for automated Scope 3 emissions reporting.

Analytical inference: The transportation sector's 29% share of total U.S. energy consumption in 2025, and diesel's 22% share of transportation energy, means that on-highway diesel price movements flowing through the EIA portal directly affect the cost baseline against which freight operators model fuel surcharges, emissions intensity per tonne-km, and renewable-fuel substitution economics.

*Source: EIA — Use of Energy for Transportation, U.S. Energy Information Administration, 2026. https://www.eia.gov/energyexplained/use-of-energy/transportation.php

Analytical inference: The inclusion of biofuel data (6% of U.S. transportation energy in 2025) within the same API infrastructure as conventional petroleum prices means the portal can, in principle, support side-by-side cost and emissions intensity comparisons for renewable diesel and biodiesel blends versus conventional diesel — a direct input to fuel-switching business cases on U.S. road lanes.

---

# 3. Supplier Access and Procurement Reality

**Access model.** The EIA API is free. Access requires registration for a free API key. EIA transmits the key automatically to the registered email address.

*Source: EIA API Technical Documentation, U.S. Energy Information Administration, March 2026. https://www.eia.gov/opendata/documentation.php

**No exclusivity or procurement barrier.** The data is a U.S. government open-data asset. There is no licensing fee, no volume tier, and no enterprise procurement process. The API is accessible to any registered user.

*Source: EIA Open Data Portal, U.S. Energy Information Administration, 2026. https://eia.gov/opendata

**Technical integration requirements.** APIv2 is a fully RESTful implementation using only HTTPS. Datasets are arranged in a logical tree hierarchy; member datasets can be discovered by querying their parent node. API returns are customisable: callers specify columns, date ranges, and facets, removing the need for client-side filtering. The API supports JSON output natively and XML output via the `out=xml` parameter. The 5,000-row maximum per query applies; pagination is managed via offset and length parameters.

*Source: EIA API Technical Documentation, U.S. Energy Information Administration, March 2026. https://www.eia.gov/opendata/documentation.php

**Known data gaps (third-party validated).** The PUDL project documents that the EIA redacts fuel price records for independent power producers (IPPs) in competitive wholesale markets — primarily the Northeastern U.S. — leaving approximately 23% of monthly fuel cost records unfillable. This gap is specific to electricity sector plant-level fuel costs and does not directly affect on-highway diesel price series, but it is evidence that the API has structural redaction patterns that analytics pipelines must account for.

*Source: PUDL/Catalyst Cooperative — EIA Bulk API Data Documentation, Catalyst Cooperative, 2026. https://catalystcoop-pudl.readthedocs.io/en/latest/data_sources/eiaapi.html

**Bulk download option.** Full bulk file download is available in addition to the API, with a documented archive (Zenodo DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.7067366). Total download size is 1,526 MB.

*Source: PUDL/Catalyst Cooperative — EIA Bulk API Data Documentation, Catalyst Cooperative, 2026. https://catalystcoop-pudl.readthedocs.io/en/latest/data_sources/eiaapi.html

---

# 4. Operational Fit by Transport Mode and Cargo Vertical

**Air (primary mode for workspace operations)**

Operational implication: The EIA portal publishes jet fuel spot and futures prices within its petroleum data series. For workspaces operating on air-primary lanes, jet fuel price series provide the underlying cost input for aviation fuel surcharge modelling and for emissions intensity benchmarking against SAF alternatives. The biofuels data series, which covers ethanol and renewable fuel production and capacity, is the adjacent data source for SAF substitution economics — though the portal does not publish SAF-specific price series as a named dataset; SAF pricing remains outside the portal's current scope. Workspaces benchmarking SAF costs against conventional jet fuel will need to source SAF pricing from industry bodies or carrier disclosures separately.

*Source: EIA Open Data Portal, U.S. Energy Information Administration, 2026. https://eia.gov/opendata

**Road (secondary mode)**

Operational implication: Weekly on-highway diesel prices — published every Monday via the Gasoline and Diesel Fuel Update — are the portal's most directly operational data series for road freight. For workspaces operating road freight on U.S. domestic lanes (including drayage and final-mile for live events, luxury goods, fine art, and high-value automotive cargo), these weekly prices are the authoritative cost baseline for fuel surcharge negotiation and Scope 3 Category 4 transport emissions calculations under ISO 14083 or GHG Protocol. State-level disaggregation via the SEDS dataset further enables per-lane emissions intensity modelling where cargo volumes justify it.

*Source: EIA Petroleum & Other Liquids Data, U.S. Energy Information Administration, June 2026. https://www.eia.gov/petroleum/data.php

Operational implication: The distillate fuel oil consumption and sales data (weekly product supplied, monthly supplier sales volumes) within the petroleum series provides demand-side context for diesel availability and price volatility — useful for workspaces planning time-sensitive road operations (live events, concert tours, film and TV production logistics) where fuel availability constraints affect delivery reliability.

**Ocean (tertiary mode)**

Operational implication: The portal includes residual fuel oil prices and consumption data, which provide a baseline input for ocean bunkering cost modelling on U.S.-originating trade lanes. However, the portal does not publish bunker price series (e.g., VLSFO, MGO) as named datasets; those are sourced from port-specific bunker indices. The EIA data supplements rather than replaces maritime fuel pricing sources for ocean-primary operators.

*Source: EIA Open Data Portal, U.S. Energy Information Administration, 2026. https://eia.gov/opendata

---

# 5. Competitive Positioning Implications for the Workspace

COMPETITIVE EDGE

**Access parity — and the differentiation is in the pipeline, not the key.**

Analytical inference: Because EIA API access is free and open with no procurement barrier, any freight operator or competitor can register and pull the same data. The competitive differentiation is not in having access — it is in having built a workflow that (a) pulls relevant series automatically, (b) maps EIA fuel and emissions data to specific trade lanes and cargo verticals, and (c) integrates the outputs into client-facing sustainability reporting. Workspaces that have built this integration can generate verifiable, government-sourced emissions baselines and fuel cost projections faster and more credibly than those relying on manual lookups or third-party data aggregators at subscription cost.

Analytical inference: For cargo verticals where clients are beginning to request Scope 3 emissions data — including live events (where tour carbon reporting is increasingly standard for major artists), luxury goods (where brand sustainability commitments are transmitted to supply chain partners), and fine art (where institutional clients face their own reporting obligations) — the ability to cite EIA-sourced emission factors and fuel baselines in client deliverables is a credible differentiator in competitive RFPs.

Analytical inference: Workspaces that have not yet integrated the EIA API into their emissions accounting workflow face a gap: they are either purchasing equivalent data from commercial data vendors (at cost) or using static, manually updated fuel price lookups (with lag and version risk). The EIA portal eliminates both costs when correctly integrated.

*Specific [named competitor EIA API integration status] not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-05.* No sourced reporting was identified confirming which named freight forwarders have or have not integrated EIA API data into their sustainability reporting workflows. Competitive positioning at the named-operator level is a research gap.

---

# 6. Conversational and Strategic Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- "Our diesel cost and emissions baselines are sourced from the U.S. Energy Information Administration's Open Data API — the same source used by U.S. government agencies and independent research institutions — updated weekly for on-highway diesel and monthly for emissions factors." *(Grounded: EIA API provides weekly diesel prices and CO₂ emissions data. Source: https://eia.gov/opendata and https://www.eia.gov/petroleum/data.php)*
- "We can provide state-level transport emissions intensity data disaggregated by fuel type, drawn from the EIA State Energy Data System." *(Grounded: SEDS covers annual state-level data by energy source and sector. Source: https://eia.gov/opendata)*
- "The EIA's 2025 data shows petroleum accounts for approximately 89% of U.S. transportation energy, with diesel at 22% — which is why our road freight emissions calculations are diesel-intensity-led, not generic transport averages." *(Grounded: EIA transportation energy data. Source: https://www.eia.gov/energyexplained/use-of-energy/transportation.php)*

**Questions to pose to clients:**
- "Your sustainability team's Scope 3 Category 4 and Category 9 calculations — are those currently using a static emission factor, or a route- and fuel-specific factor updated to the most recent reporting period? We can demonstrate the difference."
- "Do your reporting frameworks (CSRD, GHG Protocol, ISO 14083) require you to distinguish between diesel, renewable diesel, and biodiesel blend emissions factors? We can supply all three from primary government sources."

**Pitfalls to avoid:**
- Do not claim the EIA portal provides SAF-specific pricing — it does not. SAF prices are not a named series in the portal as documented in the source blocks.
- Do not claim the portal provides real-time pricing — weekly diesel updates are the highest-frequency series; they are not intraday or even daily.
- Do not claim that EIA emissions factors are the same as carrier-reported Scope 1 emissions — they are reference baselines, not operator-specific actuals. This distinction matters in any client conversation where the workspace's figures will be used in a third-party audit.

---

# 7. Time-to-Market, Procurement Window, and Action

COMPETITIVE EDGE | Re-check window: ongoing; API version updates approximately quarterly based on recent cadence.

**Availability:** The EIA Open Data Portal and APIv2 are live and free as of this brief. No procurement timeline applies.

**Specific actions for the workspace:**

1. **Register for an API key immediately** — zero cost, zero lead time. Visit https://www.eia.gov/opendata/ to register. The key is transmitted automatically by email. This is a one-time, same-day action.

2. **Identify and pull the three highest-value series for the workspace's U.S. road and air operations:**
   - Weekly on-highway diesel prices (petroleum/prices route) — for road freight cost and emissions baselines on U.S. domestic lanes.
   - Jet fuel spot prices (petroleum/prices route) — for air freight fuel cost benchmarking.
   - State-level CO₂ emissions by transportation sector (SEDS/CO₂ route) — for per-state Scope 3 emissions intensity factors.

3. **Map EIA series to the workspace's active U.S. trade lanes** — connecting EIA state-level data to the specific origin-destination pairs the workspace operates for live events, luxury, fine art, high-value automotive, and humanitarian cargo. This mapping is an internal analytical task; it does not require additional data procurement.

4. **Add EIA as a named data source in client-facing sustainability deliverables** — replacing any manually maintained or commercially purchased U.S. fuel price and emissions factor inputs. This is a reporting quality and cost-reduction action simultaneously.

5. **Brief the operations team on the STEO (Short-Term Energy Outlook)** — the 18-month forward projection model. For operations leads planning fuel surcharge structures and client contract terms on multi-month U.S. engagements, the STEO provides the only free, government-modelled forward price path. Analytical inference: this is particularly relevant for live events and film/TV productions where contracts are signed months in advance of operations.

6. **Monitor API versioning** — subscribe to EIA RSS/email updates to receive patch notes. The recent cadence (four patches in seven months) suggests the API is in active development; breaking changes are possible, and a pinned version strategy may be warranted for automated pipelines.

*Source: EIA API Technical Documentation, U.S. Energy Information Administration, March 2026. https://www.eia.gov/opendata/documentation.php

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1-7) | Why this source matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| EIA API Technical Documentation | https://www.eia.gov/opendata/documentation.php | 2 | Official EIA deep-dive on APIv2 structure, versioning history, and query syntax — essential for any workspace building an automated data pipeline against EIA series. |
| EIA — Use of Energy for Transportation | https://www.eia.gov/energyexplained/use-of-energy/transportation.php | 2 | Primary EIA source for U.S. transportation energy fuel-mix data (2025 actuals: petroleum 89%, diesel 22%, biofuels 6%); the authoritative baseline for freight emissions intensity modelling. |
| EIA Petroleum & Other Liquids Data | https://www.eia.gov/petroleum/data.php | 2 | Comprehensive petroleum data hub for weekly on-highway diesel prices, refiner product prices, consumption/sales, and biofuel capacity — core operational inputs for U.S. road freight cost and emissions calculations. |
| PUDL / Catalyst Cooperative — EIA Bulk API Data Documentation | https://catalystcoop-pudl.readthedocs.io/en/latest/data_sources/eiaapi.html | 6 | Independent third-party documentation of EIA API categories and known data gaps (redaction patterns, missing cost records); validates practical pipeline integration behaviour and surfaces limitations not disclosed in EIA's own documentation. |

---
